sentences.text: So the people-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] --who were put into the--
When we were putted-- assigned to the left and to the right, our group, that were to survive, we went to the right by foot.
And we were taken for few minutes to a barrack.
This barrack, you know, we stayed there until nighttime.
But at the same times, they send us, you know, to the right, to the left.
The people to the right were divided in two groups-- male and female.
So when we arrived to this barrack after a few-- like you say, 1/2 an hour march, we were telled to rest there for a little while.
The little while stretch out till nighttime.
Nighttime came, you know.
They brought us-- the men, as well, of our group-- for five minutes to the other side of the room to show us, I suppose, who were the survivor.
Because we didn't know at the time what it meant.
It was a very dark room.
They put the men from one side and the women to the other side.
That is, the women and men that they were waiting to survive-- that they were going to go to the labor camp.
They switched it on the light, for five minutes, and we can see each other.
After five minutes, the lights were back again dark, and the men were taken away.
And we women were told to go-- but still, you know, the whole day without food-- to go to another room.
The first thing we do-- we did during that night-- that time-- it was already 2 o'clock at night-- they took us to a place where they were prisoners-- barbers, or whatever you call it-- with shavers.
And they were shaving us, all the hair from our hair and all the bodies.
There was other womans with some DDT-- with, you know, like you pump an animal-- all over us, to disinfect us.
And then, you know, they say, you must go to block number 20.
The-- the rooms-- I mean, the blocks, they had-- the shed-- the-- in the camp, they were named by blocks.
They were called blocks.
We were told to go to block number 20.
So, though we were upset and everything, when they shaved our hair-- but it-- we looked so ridiculous-- what we start laughing each other.
Because each one look like his brother-- like her brother.
Because, you know, everybody had brothers and sister.
But with no hair, we thought we were talking to the brothers of the-- of the girls.
Eventually, you know, still we know we were very upset.
And we were taken to block number 20, which were occupied by other prisoner.
And they told us, go and sleep wherever you want.
Wherever you want-- it was nowhere to be-- you know, to sleep.
And there were-- the-- the block 20 was full of prisoner like us.
And it was dark, very dark.
It was like 3 o'clock, or 4 o'clock in the morning.
And we couldn't distinguish clearly who was in there, you know, in the planks.
They sleeping.
Us-- nobody had hair like us.
And we thought they were all men, you know-- male people.
And then he say, girls-- because we're all speaking Spanish-- nobody go to sleep in those beds, because they're all-- all men sleeping there.
But eventually, they were all girls.
But we didn't realize, you know, that they were no hair like us.
Come morning time, and came, you know, the [NON-ENGLISH], which means the chief of the block, which usually, she was also a prisoner-- usually, always.
And-- but she had already staying in the camp for a few months.
And she had more experience.
And they were appointed by the Germans to be-- to be, you know, in charge of the blocks.
So now, this [NON-ENGLISH], the woman [NON-ENGLISH], they were very good.
But it happened, you know, that in the men-- in the men camp, we heard, afterwards, the kapos and [NON-ENGLISH] were horrible.
They were prisoner like them, but they were like savages.
Actually, the Germans came only in the morning to count us-- and in the evening.
So during the day, if they had wanted to be more human to their fellow prisoner, they could have done with-- nobody will ever witness it.
But they were all like savages, you know, because they were already so many months ahead of us.
And life, it was so bad there that you cannot reason anymore what you were doing.
You were not any-- you can't differentiate good to evil.
Because they were witnessing bad things about, you know, the Germans.
And they were maybe learning.
I don't know what it was.

And beside that, at the-- from the very moment we arrive, till at the end, the-- the food that they give us, the little food that they give us-- like bread and-- and soup-- you know, that they're giving us, they was full of bromo, which was a substance to-- to make you, you know, like imbecile.
We were not-- because in normal times, when you know you know something happening to-- to you, you rebel.
But there, you know, nobody rebel.
Because we were taken for granted-- like we were inferior-- that we have to do all these thing.
Because all our-- or-- our dignity was gone.
We told, there's bromo in our-- in our food.
And besides all these things, also all the woman, they never had the-- the-- their periods.
Because otherwise, how can you have in there, you know, all these millions of people?
So that is to stop.
And many of them, you know, after the war, they never had any children, on account we know they had too many months eating of this bromo.
And they were ruined for lives.
Many people today, in Israel-- but they-- thank God, in our group, nothing happened-- only a couple that didn't have children.
Because we only stay in captivity about 10 months.
But other people that are still-- like from Poland, from Holland, they were before us taken by the Germans, they were just, you know, for life like that.
Anyhow, I was saying, we arrive in those blocks without food, because the time for the food was already-- distribution of the food was gone.
So it were we have to wait for the following day.
So the next morning, when they took us to the-- to the out, you know, in the air, at 4 o'clock, usually every night-- it was when you arrive at the blocks.
It was 3 o'clock, 1/2 past 3:00 at night.
We were just trying to accommodate in the floor, whatever, to find a little sleep after, you know, so-- such a long day.
4 o'clock, they start, you know, screaming-- out, out, out, in German.
[SPEAKING GERMAN]!
It means, that's the time has come to be-- you know, the-- the-- the time to call you, you know, for the counting.
Actually, they call us 4 o'clock every single night.
The German will come only at 8 o'clock in the morning to count us.
But from 4 o'clock, you go out.
You go into the latrines.
The latrines consisted of a big, big pit-- a hole-- with a plank with some holes.
That's it.
And then, only a minute for a person, you know.
Because there were so many that they want to be there.
It was just, you know, like planks with holes, and you have to be there-- only a minute.
If you stay a little bit longer, there were-- this-- the girls in charge, with a big stick.
They will plant the stick into the, you know, his excrement, and they will put in your mouth-- and if you don't come out of the toilet in time.
So everybody was [GERMAN]!
We have to go there, outside.
So after this thing, you have to go to the-- like, clean your face, or whatever, or drink water, because you were very thirsty.
And the-- the place where you can drink the water, it was a big, you know, open space full of pipes.
Now and then, there were holes.
There were not even taps-- holes in the pipe-- some coming-- some water coming out of this pipe.
And you have to go there.
And then, [INAUDIBLE] this time, you know, it was just about 20 minutes for all these hundreds of people, you know, who wanted to go there.
It was no-- not even a queue-- one jumping on top of the other one to go and reach the water.
So many times, you know, by the time they call you, it's finished.
The time is finished.
You haven't even reach next to the water.
You have to stay until the next day without water another night.
You see, one day, I remember I stay.
I was very, very thirsty.
And I was approaching the-- the pipes.
It was my turn to have a drink of water.
So time cames, and the German-- you know, I just starting, putting my mouth into the water.
The German just blew his whistle-- time is up.
And then myself, I didn't care.
I say, never mind.
I'm too thirsty.
There was so much thirst, I didn't care.
And the German came with a-- with a-- with a stick, and he hurt me very, very badly.
All the time of the camp, I had here, also-- even now, you know, after so many years, I got still the-- the scar.
Because he hit me so badly, it got infected.
But I never complained or showed to the-- to the-- to the, you know, to the [NON-ENGLISH] that I was not feeling well, or that I had temperature, or whatever.
Because if you go to the revere, which is a hospital, that, it was the end-- for sure.
They will never-- nobody entered the hospital and come back alive.
Everybody who entered the hospital was dead.
Because whatever disease you had, you were sick or not sick-- but once you were in that hospital, during the night, you know, they give you a pill.
I don't know what.
It must be some poison, you know?
And in the morning, you were dead in your-- in your bed.
They would keep you just one night in hospital.
The next day, they were coming-- the lorries-- and taking you to-- to the crematorium.
Because you were dead-- definitely.
So now it happens that my sister, who was with me-- and usually, you know, she's in Rhodes Island.
She was plumper than me.
I was all the time, you know, thin, in Rhodes Island-- but she was plumper than me.
And she weighed a few more kilos than me.
And in the camp, she become like a skeleton.
Whenever she was sitting, you know, in the floor, the blood was coming out of her hips, you know, because it was no-- no-- no flesh.
It was just bones and-- and skin.
So one day, you know, she had enough.
She couldn't resist anymore.
It was so cold.
Imagine.
We, you know, we live in Rhodes Island.
We never saw the-- the-- the snow in our life.
We never saw temperature going below 15 degrees, or whatever.
So imagine, all of a sudden, from Rhodes Island-- so a climate, Mediterranean climate-- to go to these places there, which was under zero.
When, you know, we arrive, it was very nice-- all right.
Because it was August.
And it was very, very hot.
But after August, come September and October.
So it was snowing.
The first time we saw the snow, we all started crying-- like it was, you know, something unbelievable.
And we thought we are going to die for sure when we saw the camp.
So in any case, when-- I told you.
It was the time, you know, for the appell, that night, you know.
It was the block 20.
The next morning, we were taken out into the air.
There was-- there were girls starting with the tattooing our-- our arms.
They were sitting on a chair.
And next to them, they had the pencils and the-- like a little stick, like a little pen with-- but it wasn't really very-- very painful.
So they started putting, you know, like-- like a point-- they point at each number.
It's got a point, you know, making the-- the-- the-- the alphabet-- first, the letter-- which group you belong.
Which mine, in that case, it was the A; and then after, making your number.
The number that I bear is 24283.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
So after, you know, they told us to keep the-- the-- the-- the arm outstretch until the ink will dry.
And then the name-- the number, you know, will show very-- very strongly.
But myself, when they finish tattooing my arm, I went and I-- I rub it against my thing, because it-- I-- it was so ugly.
And then today, it is one of the faintest number of all the other people-- they are from-- in my group.
Everybody was scared.
When they saw my-- my number like that, they say, be careful.
Even the German girls, when they-- the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] told me.
Be careful not to show to the Germans your number.
If they see it so fainted, they will kill you.
Because-- but I-- I didn't realize.
When you are young, you don't know what-- you know, you don't fear danger.
Anyhow, when the-- the-- the-- the thing was done, they took us, you know, to the-- to the blocks.
And it was, you know, we-- they took us to the-- no.
I told you that they took us to be counted.
The-- the counting will be only-- will be only at 8 o'clock in the morning, with the German coming at 8 o'clock.
But between 5:00-- like we finish the toilet, and the-- the water, and everything.
From 5:00 to 8:00, we have to stand in the-- in rows, like in a-- in an army-- five in a rows.
That it was their system-- five in a rows-- you know, the men, the woman, whatever they were doing, it's-- it was in five-- five in a row.
And when it was, you know, at the beginning, it was warm.
But when it was, as I says, October, November, it was very, very cold-- for three hours standing there.
Because we had no underwear.
We had no shoes.
We had no hair.
And we had given one piece-- one item of-- of-- of clothing each.
It could be a men's shirt.
It could be a trouser.
It could be whatever there.
It was in a pile.
When you were, you know, there, they-- they undress us.
And you have to pick-- running, one-- whatever comes to you.
Like, it could be one girl tall, tall with a small, small blouse; one-- one girl very short with a-- with an evening dress.
Whatever comes, it was your-- your luck.
And you have to pick an item.
And you have to keep the item forever.
Day and night, like you cannot undress during the night-- the same item.
So we were full of lice.
You know, what do you expect?
You know, no washing, not everything-- we have this-- for it was full of lice.
So-- What did you choose when you picked?
Whatever-- it was there-- the first one to come.
Whatever--
When I arrived, it was not always the same.
Because you choose one.
And it was-- it was the first time I choose.
It was August.
And it was a very, very heavy dress that I was perspiring day and night, you know, with that black dress.
I hate it.
The second time, you know, after, like, a few weeks, they take you to the showers.
To the showers-- they give you a small bar of soap when you enter.
And you go to the showers.
In the-- in the-- in the ceiling, you know, there were-- you know, sometimes it was water coming through.
Sometimes, it was gas.
So now, we went-- enter in this knowing.
Because sometimes we will die.
Sometime, we will come back alive.
And before entering, no-- no-- no towels or anything.
They will give you a bar of soap.
In this bar of soap, there were three initials-- in Germans.
That, I forgot-- how to pronounce in German.
But it means pure Jewish fat-- into the soap.
It was-- it meant that they were making the soap out of the corpse of the living.
They make those soaps.
They were making out of the-- of the corpse of the living, many things.
They will make, you know, that parchment, to make that-- to-- to-- to the lampshades.
They will make, you know, purses for them.
For the skin of the dead people, they will make you beautiful skins, you know, like purses for the Germans.
They will make anything.

The-- and then, the-- the-- the flesh, you know, they were-- sometimes, you know, when they burn-- if they were not burned, they will use it for-- to make sausages, and to make, you know, bologna-- that they were giving us at night to eat.
It was you know, like you say me-- at the beginning we were very kosher.
Where as we say in Rhodes Island, we didn't want to touch bologna-- not even knowing that it was human.
We thought it was from the cow.
But then, you know, my father, want-- at the beginning-- 40 days, we will see the men, you know, from the other side of the fence.
There were electric barbed, you know, wire.
And then my father-- that he was very well-- I mean, he knew very well, you know, the Hebrew, and all these laws, and all this.
He told us in Hebrew, [HEBREW], which means the law of the government is the law.
So if you don't eat, you'll starve, and you'll die.
So God knows that.
So don't-- don't-- forget about Kashrut and everything.
Eat whatever they give you.
So at the beginning, you know, we didn't want to-- even that we knew that, when we didn't want to touch that bologna.
But after that, never mind, you know, we-- we ated anything they will give us-- especially when it come winter-- you know, it was very, very cold.
And the whole-- and the whole, you know, horizon, we can see wherever you go, it was snow.
There was not a single-- a single grass growing nowhere.
It was all snow-- snow, snow, snow everywhere-- all white.
And it was, you know, deep into the snow that we walked without shoes.
And many people, you know, while they were standing in the queue, I say, for three hours-- to be counted, they were frozen.
And they will not-- they will die there on the spot.
And sometimes, you know, their feet, you know, they can get frozen.
They cannot move anymore.
And then they can-- they can be killed all the same.
So in any case, at the beginning, you know, I say, it was warm.
It wasn't so bad to be in this queue.
But after that, when it was very cold, and we couldn't-- we couldn't stay, you know, for three hours.
And we were trying, you know, to be, you know, against each other-- the bodies of warm body, to give little bit of warm to another body in front of us, on the other side.
Because you have to be five in a row.
And come this [NON-ENGLISH] with a stick.
And we have to keep a distance from one to another person, like, at least one meter.
Like that-- the wind, and the cold, and the snow will come to you in all direction-- in your face, in your [? everything. ?] Imagine-- no hair; into your ears-- it was cold, cold, cold-- like that.
I can't imagine how much cold.
And still, we were enduring.
We couldn't do nothing else.
But people were dying like flies.
Beside, you know, every week-- every week-- you know, and not every week-- every three days, coming the German there to have the selection.
The selection means taking-- for instance, they wanted to die for-- today for the-- for their crematorium, so many thousands of people.
They will grab at random.
And they take it from the block 20 so many-- from block 20, until they reach their number to be cremated and asphyxiated.
So-- otherwise, you were also dying for-- from-- from diseases, which it was cholera.
It was very much there-- cholera, typhus, and sort of disease of the skin.
And then, beside that, you know, whole day, the-- from the very first moment we arrive in the camp, it was a very, very bad smell into the air, that it was impregnated everywhere.
Whatever you touch, whatever you drink, it was like burning flesh.
That even now, you know, after 50 years, I got it still.
I can feel it in my nose-- that burning, like a very bad smell of this burning flesh, human flesh.
Anyhow, after this-- this thing, we were in quarantine for 40 days, because we were the arrived-- we arrive from-- from the trains.
And we had all sort of, you know, germs on us.
For 40 days, nobody was-- you know, beside being, you know, selected for crematorium, they didn't gave us a bad-- I mean, a-- a very consistent job to do in the camp.
But they make-- they make us, you know, do things unbelievable, just to-- to-- to reduce us without any strength.
For instance, one morning, they will say to you-- you see, there were piles of bricks there in the-- in the-- in the camp.
You must take three, four bricks-- they were very heavy-- in each-- in each hand.
And you must go by walk, and then the Germans will follow us.
By-- they were coming by jeeps, or whatever, to deposit those bricks so many miles away in the camp, which we were, you know-- we have to do it.
And we [INAUDIBLE].
And then, when we were just carrying those bricks, the German would also tell us, you must sing.
Because when we were transporting those bricks, we have to cross, you know, paths that come out of the camp.
And the-- and the population, they will hear our voices.
And they will say, there are the prisoner.
They are very happy.
They're singing.
And then, they knew that-- they knew that we were Italians.
And they invariably, every day they want us to sing 'Mama,' because it was a song that has come in, you know, in vogue at this very moment.
And then, they liked that song.

And they want us to sing every morning when we're carrying these bricks-- very, very heavy-- to the place what they were, you know, designated-- and to sing 'Mama.' We had not even strength to walk, never mind to sing.
But we were doing all the same.
So the next day, now, we were ordered to take squares of lawn-- very, very happy-- to be-- carry the same place where we have, the previous day-- we have putted the bricks.
So we were carrying those pieces of lawn to that place where the bricks were.
And then, bring it back-- those bricks that we have taken the day before.
It was really no sense what we were doing.
It's just, you know, to make us weak, and demoralized, and thing.
We were, you know, doing those things.
And then, we were-- already know, at night, you know, they were giving us a piece of bread.
And the-- the-- the-- as we were-- you know, again, at night, we were also counted.
We were counted in the morning at 8 o'clock, and in the evening at 6 o'clock-- in very-- every day.
So from 5 o'clock, we have to be in the-- in the queues again.
The German will count if it were all the same, you know, number.
So-- and then we-- after they-- we'd been counted, the German, the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] will come with one small bread-- brown bread-- stale, and everything.
And they give to the first person in the row that brick of brown bread-- stale and hard.
But we were awaiting impatiently-- never mind.
And it tasted the best food in the world, because we were so hungry.
And he was-- she was given that piece, but it was already cut in five pieces.
So this little girl here, she has to take that piece and distribute to all the girls behind her in five pieces.
It was-- call it, like maybe 100 grams of bread for the whole 24 hours.
That, it was.
And then, there was a little bit of soup.
The little bit of soap would consist, you know, like some cabbages, and potatoes with the skin, and the-- and the-- and the rubbish-- all inside without being wash.
And then, you know, they were going to give us a [NON-ENGLISH], which it means a little container to each one.
Many times, you know, we-- we can't even eat that soup, because it was finish.
Because we were-- you know, as I say, we were from the Mediterranean.
We didn't know how to speak the language.
We didn't know German.
We didn't know anything.
We were always the last to get whatever it was being distributed.
So we were happy-- we were lucky that we were with my sister at the time-- and my other sister-- the three sisters.
So we made a plan.
Because many girls, they were on their own.
They will take the bread, 100 grams, just a piece of bread like that.
They will eat the bread.
And the whole day long, the next day, they will be without food until the next distribution.
And they were starving.
They were really starving.
And then, we were very lucky, as we were three sister.
And we make a plan.
We divided the three portions in three meals.
The first meal we'll have a piece each from these three.
Because we were given three pieces, three portions.
The-- the night, we will have one portion.
For breakfast, we will have the portion of the other one.
And the lunch, we'll have the other portion.
Like that, we had something to chew and keep us going until the next-- the next time of the distribution.
And then, you know, occasionally, once a month-- it was a Sunday-- they will give us small potatoes-- boiled for lunch.
That, it was luxury, like you were getting the best meal in the world.
And then they say, today is the day of the potatoes.
People, they were-- you know, used to have in their houses the best meal, whatever you say.
And they're waiting there.
And they, you know, they was taking a container and giving to your own with-- with the skin and the-- and the-- and the dirty, whatever, all inside.
They never wash whatever it was.
And then, you know, before we can eat, we used to keep those potatoes for a few hours, counting them.
How many potatoes have you got?
And then she will say, oh, I've got five.
And you?
The-- four.
Oh, I'm lucky.
I've got only four potatoes.
You got five.
That it was like you had, you know, treasure, counting the millions in the bank.
That it was our own, you know, enjoyment counting the potatoes.
Eventually, we will eat those potatoes until the next distribution.
And then, it was nighttime again.
It was all the stories-- all the stories again.
And-- and after these meals, you know, if-- many girls, they try to go to the kitchens to steal something, you know, to eat.
Many, they enter, and you know, they're more clever or not.
Because the more you know-- not clever.
You must say they were, and they were starving the most.
[BACKROUND NOISE] If they find you pinching a potato, or a piece of bread, or whatever, or an onion, whatever, they-- and they were to find you-- and it was very much punishment.
They will-- they will kill you on the spot.
And beside that, you know, there were other punishment they could do to you.
They were-- especially, there were, you know, those dogs.
There was those dogs in the camp.
They were very huge dogs.
We were afraid of those dogs more than the-- the Germans.
Because they were train to make, you know, to make like sex with girls.
So if they want to punish you, they will take these horrible, big dogs, and they force you there to make whatever.
In any case, one day, it happened there was a-- a-- a French lady.
And she had a beautiful daughter of 16.
She was really beautiful.
And then, you know, they-- they-- they forced her, the girl-- I don't know what she has done wrong-- to do that with the dog.
The mother was-- was watching.
The-- the-- the girl died, because the dog was vicious, and bad, and big, and whatever.
She die on the spot.
And the mother, she couldn't stand it anymore.
She went and hanged herself to the barbed wire, you know, electric.
And she die as well.
And the-- the-- beside all this horror, there were also those in the towers, those German watching you-- every movement you did in the camp.
Anything it was no-- not clear to them, they will shoot on the spot.
[INAUDIBLE] dead, count-- nothing.
You know?
It was the-- you know, a fly, you know, in human time-- you know, in normal time-- it's got more chance to survive than us.
Excuse me.
All right.
